findings.summary: Circular dependency chain between engine._scoring, engine._state, and intelligence.review requires 4+ deferred imports
evidence: engine._scoring.subjective.core has 3 deferred imports from intelligence.review.dimensions (lines 64, 76, 99) with comment: 'Deferred import to avoid circular: scoring -> _scoring/results/core -> _scoring/subjective/core -> intelligence.review -> state -> scoring'
engine._state.scoring has deferred import from desloppify.scoring (line 288) and from intelligence.review.dimensions.data (line 329)
engine._state.filtering has deferred import from engine._state.scoring (line 124)
intelligence.review.importing.holistic has deferred imports from engine.concerns (lines 305, 384)
findings.suggestion: Extract dimension metadata (display names, weights, holistic dimension list) into a leaf module under engine._scoring or a new core.dimensions module that both engine._scoring and intelligence.review can import without cycles. The dimension data is static configuration, not runtime logic, and should not require the scoring pipeline to reach it.

findings.summary: engine.planning.dimension_rows imports app.output.scorecard_parts.dimensions via deferred import, violating layer direction
evidence: Line 34 of dimension_rows.py: 'from desloppify.app.output.scorecard_parts.dimensions import prepare_scorecard_dimensions' inside a try/except with comment 'deferred import to avoid hard engine -> app dependency'
The engine layer should never depend on the app/presentation layer, even optionally. This creates invisible upward coupling.
findings.suggestion: Move the shared scorecard dimension row logic into engine.planning or engine._scoring so both engine and app can consume it. The app layer should call down into engine, not the other way around.

findings.summary: desloppify/search.py duplicates desloppify/core/grep.py nearly line-for-line, creating a dual-source boundary
evidence: search.py (77 LOC) contains read_file_text, grep_files, grep_files_containing, grep_count_files with identical signatures and logic to core/grep.py (81 LOC)
search.py uses PROJECT_ROOT constant directly; core/grep.py uses _get_project_root() function -- subtle divergence in path resolution behavior
search.py is only imported by tests/core/test_search_versioning.py; core/grep.py is imported by utils.py and multiple typescript detectors
findings.suggestion: Delete search.py and redirect its test imports to core.grep. The file was left behind during the core/ extraction and serves no distinct purpose.

findings.summary: Deprecated compatibility facades (utils.py, file_discovery.py) still exist with no active external consumers beyond tests
evidence: utils.py imports from 6 different core submodules and re-exports ~40 symbols; file_discovery.py re-exports ~15 symbols
Both emit DeprecationWarning at import time with planned removal 2026-09-30
All non-test callers have migrated: only test files still import from these facades (7 test files for utils, 1 for file_discovery)
These facades increase the public surface area and create confusing dual import paths
findings.suggestion: Update the remaining 8 test files to import from the canonical core submodules, then delete both compatibility facades. The 2026-09-30 removal date is approaching; clearing them now avoids forgetting.

findings.summary: app/commands/review/ has 18 Python files totaling ~7000 LOC with mixed concerns: orchestration, prompt templates, scoring, importing
evidence: runner_helpers.py alone is 1213 LOC with 30+ functions and 6 classes, mixing subprocess execution, file I/O, parallel scheduling, progress tracking, and packet sanitization
batch_core.py (753 LOC) handles finding normalization, merging, and quality validation
batches.py (812 LOC) handles batch execution flow orchestration
The distinction between runner_helpers, batches, batch, and batch_core is unclear without reading the code
findings.suggestion: Split runner_helpers.py into focused submodules: runner_execution (subprocess + popen), runner_progress (progress events + live output), and packet_sanitization (blind packet building + SHA). Consider a review/batch/ subpackage for batch_core, batch_prompt_template, batch_scoring, and batches.

findings.summary: Scan reporting is split across 7 files with an unclear naming convention (scan_reporting_{analysis,dimensions,llm,presentation,subjective,summary,text})
evidence: All 7 files share the scan_reporting_ prefix but the suffix names overlap conceptually: 'analysis' vs 'summary', 'presentation' vs 'text', 'dimensions' vs 'subjective'
Cross-imports exist: scan_reporting_summary imports from scan_reporting_llm, scan_reporting_dimensions imports from scan_reporting_presentation and scan_reporting_subjective
A new developer cannot predict which file a given reporting function lives in from the name alone
findings.suggestion: Create a scan/reporting/ subpackage and rename files by output audience: terminal_output, agent_context (LLM block), dimension_table, integrity_report. The conceptual grouping should be by what is rendered, not the type of data.

findings.summary: PROJECT_ROOT is computed from env var/cwd at module import time, requiring RuntimeContext workarounds for testing
evidence: text_utils.py line 10: _DEFAULT_PROJECT_ROOT = Path(os.environ.get('DESLOPPIFY_ROOT', Path.cwd())).resolve() -- runs at import time, captures cwd of import moment
paths_api.py lines 13-17: PROJECT_ROOT, DEFAULT_PATH, SRC_PATH are all computed from this import-time value
cli.py line 26: 'PROJECT_ROOT = get_project_root()' with _PROJECT_ROOT_SENTINEL pattern to detect test patches
RuntimeContext.project_root exists specifically as a workaround so tests can override the import-time value
findings.suggestion: Defer PROJECT_ROOT computation to first access via a lazy descriptor or make get_project_root() the sole API. The sentinel-comparison pattern in paths_api.py (comparing against _PROJECT_ROOT_SENTINEL) is fragile and would be unnecessary if the root were always resolved at call time.

findings.summary: core/registry.py exposes global mutable DETECTORS dict with register_detector() mutating module-level state and rebuilding JUDGMENT_DETECTORS
evidence: DETECTORS dict is mutable at module scope (line 59); register_detector() mutates it and reassigns global JUDGMENT_DETECTORS (line 342-348)
cli.py line 44 registers callback via on_detector_registered() to invalidate a global cache
registry_state.py has parallel pattern: module-level _registry dict, _load_attempted flag, _load_errors dict, all mutated via functions with 'global' statements
reset_registered_detectors() exists specifically for test cleanup, indicating the global state causes test isolation problems
findings.suggestion: Encapsulate detector registry state in a DetectorRegistry class that can be instantiated per test. Provide a default module-level instance for production use but allow test fixtures to create isolated instances.

findings.summary: CONFIG_FILE constant in core/config.py calls _default_config_file() at import time, capturing the project root at that moment
evidence: Line 23: CONFIG_FILE = _default_config_file() evaluates Path resolution at import time
Comment says 'Legacy export for call sites/tests that introspect this module constant' -- yet the function _default_config_file() exists to resolve at call time
If the project root changes after import (via RuntimeContext override), the CONFIG_FILE constant is stale
findings.suggestion: Remove the module-level CONFIG_FILE constant and have all callers use _default_config_file() or load_config() which already does the right thing. Tests that check CONFIG_FILE should check the function return value instead.

findings.summary: runner_helpers.py (1213 LOC) mixes subprocess management, parallel execution, progress reporting, file hashing, and packet sanitization
evidence: Contains 6 dataclasses (CodexBatchRunnerDeps, FollowupScanDeps, BatchResult, BatchProgressEvent, _RunnerState, _AttemptContext, _ExecutionResult) for unrelated concerns

codex_batch_command() builds shell commands; _run_via_popen() manages subprocess lifecycle; _execute_parallel() handles ThreadPoolExecutor; build_blind_packet() sanitizes data; sha256_file() does file hashing

The _BLIND_PACKET_DROP_KEYS, _BLIND_CONFIG_SCORE_HINT_KEYS, _TRANSIENT_RUNNER_PHRASES, _CODEX_BACKEND_PATH_HINT constants at module top (lines 19-60) serve entirely different subsystems within the same file
findings.suggestion: Extract into 3 focused modules: runner_process.py (subprocess + popen + stall detection), runner_parallel.py (ThreadPoolExecutor orchestration + progress), and runner_packets.py (blind packet building + SHA + provenance). Each module would have a single, clear responsibility.

findings.summary: synthesize_handlers.py (1028 LOC) contains the entire 4-stage synthesis workflow in one file with 130+ line functions
evidence: _cmd_stage_observe spans ~80 lines, _cmd_stage_reflect spans ~140 lines, _cmd_stage_organize spans ~128 lines
_cmd_synthesize_complete spans ~130 lines including inline parsing, validation, and plan mutation
Each stage function mixes input validation, data loading, user prompting, plan mutation, and output rendering
findings.suggestion: Extract each synthesis stage into its own module under plan/synthesis/ or make the stage functions smaller by extracting the plan-mutation and rendering logic into helpers. At minimum, _cmd_stage_reflect and _cmd_stage_organize each deserve their own file given their complexity.

findings.summary: import_helpers.py (981 LOC) mixes payload parsing/validation, assessment policy, provenance tracking, and CLI output rendering
evidence: 27 functions spanning distinct domains: _normalize_import_payload_shape (parsing), _assessment_provenance_status (trust evaluation), _validate_holistic_findings_schema (schema validation), print_assessment_mode_banner (CLI output), print_review_import_scores_and_integrity (rendering)
Multiple classes and types defined inline: ImportPayloadLoadError, inline policy resolution
Callers must read through 981 lines to find the function they need
findings.suggestion: Split into import_parse.py (payload normalization and schema validation), import_policy.py (assessment provenance and trust evaluation), and import_output.py (CLI printing and summaries).

findings.summary: LangRun class has 11 property pairs (getter+setter) that mechanically forward to LangRuntimeState fields
evidence: Lines 166-252: 11 property definitions each following identical pattern: @property getter returns self.state.field, @setter assigns self.state.field = value
The __getattr__ on line 254 already handles forwarding for config attributes via _FORWARDED_CONFIG_ATTRS
The properties add ~90 lines of pure boilerplate with no logic beyond delegation
findings.suggestion: Use __getattr__/__setattr__ for runtime state fields too, similar to how config attributes are already forwarded. Alternatively, make LangRuntimeState fields directly accessible via LangRun.state and document that as the access path.

findings.summary: core/ contains 3 empty subdirectories (discovery/, io/, pathing/) with only __pycache__
evidence: Each directory contains only __pycache__/ -- no Python source files
These appear to be remnants of a prior reorganization where code was moved out but the directories were not cleaned up
findings.suggestion: Delete the empty directories and their __pycache__ contents.

findings.summary: Package root has 6 non-init files (utils.py, file_discovery.py, search.py, scoring.py, state.py, versioning.py) that belong in core/ or engine/
evidence: utils.py and file_discovery.py are deprecated facades that should be deleted (see compatibility_facade_persistence finding)
search.py is a duplicate of core/grep.py (see search_grep_duplication finding)
versioning.py (43 LOC) provides compute_tool_hash() and check_tool_staleness() -- already duplicated in core/tooling.py
hook_registry.py (68 LOC) manages language detector hooks with global mutable state; its single consumer pattern (engine detectors) makes it a candidate for engine/ placement
findings.suggestion: Delete utils.py, file_discovery.py, search.py after migrating test imports. Move hook_registry.py to engine/ or languages/_framework/. Verify versioning.py is still needed given core/tooling.py exists.

findings.summary: Output helpers exist in 3 files: core/output.py (implementation), core/output_api.py (re-export facade), and core/output_contract.py (single dataclass)
evidence: core/output_api.py is a pure re-export of everything from core/output.py with zero additional logic
core/output_contract.py contains only a single 6-line dataclass (OutputResult)
Callers must decide between importing from output, output_api, or output_contract -- all in the same directory
findings.suggestion: Collapse output_api.py into output.py (or vice versa) and merge output_contract.py into output.py. Having 3 modules for output helpers in a single package with no distinct responsibilities is confusing.

findings.summary: Two separate 'importing' packages exist for review: app/commands/review/importing/ and intelligence/review/importing/
evidence: app/commands/review/importing/ has 2 files: __init__.py and modes.py (94 LOC total, handles import mode normalization)
intelligence/review/importing/ has 4 files: contracts.py, holistic.py, per_file.py, shared.py (combined ~1100 LOC, handles actual finding import logic)
A developer searching for 'review importing' code must check both locations
The app-layer importing package is small enough to not warrant its own subpackage -- modes.py could live directly in app/commands/review/
findings.suggestion: Move modes.py to app/commands/review/import_modes.py (or inline it into import_cmd.py since it is only 94 LOC) and delete the app/commands/review/importing/ subpackage. This eliminates the confusing parallel directory names.

findings.summary: core/discovery_api.py is a pure re-export facade over core/file_paths.py and core/source_discovery.py with zero added logic
evidence: discovery_api.py (48 LOC) imports and re-exports ~17 symbols from file_paths and source_discovery
Multiple modules import from discovery_api: cli.py, scan_workflow.py, override_handlers.py -- adding one layer of indirection
The original motivation (utils.py refactoring) has been served; both underlying modules have clean APIs
findings.suggestion: Have callers import directly from core.file_paths or core.source_discovery. The discovery_api facade was useful during migration but now adds a layer of indirection without value. At minimum, document it as the stable public API and deprecate direct imports from the underlying modules, or remove it.

findings.summary: Three consecutive branches (is_stale, cli_keys, else) execute identical code in build_subjective_items
evidence: Lines 310-324: the elif is_stale, elif cli_keys, and else branches all call _prepare_command(cli_keys, force_review_rerun=True) with identical arguments. Only the first two branches (open_review > 0, is_unassessed) differ.
findings.suggestion: Collapse the three identical branches into a single else clause: `else: primary_command = _prepare_command(cli_keys, force_review_rerun=True)`. This eliminates dead conditional logic and makes the two meaningful conditions (open_review, is_unassessed) stand out clearly.

findings.summary: is_subjective_finding uses verbose if/return True pattern instead of direct boolean return
evidence: Lines 36-42: `if detector in {'subjective_assessment'}: return True; if detector == 'holistic_review': return True; return False` could be a single `return detector in {...}`. Also, `in {'subjective_assessment'}` using a set literal for a single element is equivalent to `== 'subjective_assessment'`.
findings.suggestion: Replace with `return detector in {'subjective_assessment', 'holistic_review'}` for clarity and consistency with how `is_review_finding` is structured as a single return expression.

findings.summary: PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS tuple catches 7 exception types including generic ImportError, TypeError, and KeyError
evidence: PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS = (ImportError, AttributeError, OSError, ValueError, TypeError, KeyError) catches nearly every common exception type. Used in at least 10 call sites across scan_workflow.py, scan_preflight.py, queue_progress.py, and next.py. A genuine TypeError or KeyError bug in plan loading would be silently swallowed, making debugging difficult.
findings.suggestion: Define a dedicated PlanLoadError exception and catch it specifically. Have load_plan wrap its internal failures into PlanLoadError with the original cause attached. This preserves context and avoids masking programming errors.

findings.summary: 17 occurrences of `_ = exc` pattern silently discard exception context without logging
evidence: Pattern `except SomeError as exc: _ = exc` appears in 17 places (grep results show all). This swallows errors without logging. Examples: _action_type_for_detector (core.py:207) catches ImportError silently; _clear_needs_rescan_flag (scan_workflow.py:81) catches OSError silently; _try_expand_cluster (apply.py:25) catches 4 exception types silently.
findings.suggestion: Replace with `logging.debug(...)` at minimum. The `_ = exc` idiom exists only to suppress linter warnings about unused variables, but the exceptions genuinely warrant debug-level logging for operational visibility.

findings.summary: do_prepare() has 4 untyped parameters on a public API function
evidence: Line 19-26: `def do_prepare(args, state, lang, _state_path, *, config: dict) -> None` — args, state, lang, and _state_path lack type annotations. This is a public API function (exported in __all__). The function accesses args.path, state.get(), lang (passed to setup_lang_concrete), making their expected types unclear without reading the implementation.
findings.suggestion: Annotate as `def do_prepare(args: argparse.Namespace, state: StateModel, lang: LangRun | None, _state_path: Path | None, *, config: dict) -> None`. Other command entrypoints like do_merge in merge.py properly annotate args.

findings.summary: Several intelligence/review functions type lang parameter as `object` instead of LangRun
evidence: prepare.py lines 99-103: `_collect_allowed_review_files(files, lang: object)`, line 222: `prepare_review(path, lang: object, state)`, line 345: `prepare_holistic_review(path, lang: object, state)`. selection.py line 26: `select_holistic_files(path, lang: object)`. These functions access lang.file_finder, lang.zone_map, lang.dep_graph, lang.name — all LangRun attributes. Typing as `object` provides no static checking.
findings.suggestion: Type these as `lang: LangRun | LangConfig` or at minimum `lang: LangRuntimeContract` from the runtime module. Use TYPE_CHECKING import to avoid circular imports if needed.

findings.summary: LangRunOverrides fields typed as Optional unions but default to non-None sentinel object
evidence: Lines 44-57: Fields like `zone_map: FileZoneMap | None = _UNSET` and `dep_graph: dict[...] | None = _UNSET` are typed as Optional but defaulted to `_UNSET = object()` which is neither FileZoneMap, dict, nor None. Type checkers cannot verify the sentinel pattern. At line 320, `if value is _UNSET: continue` works at runtime but `_UNSET` is typed as `object` which passes the type union trivially.
findings.suggestion: Use a dedicated sentinel type: `class _Unset: pass; _UNSET = _Unset()` and type fields as `zone_map: FileZoneMap | None | _Unset = _UNSET`. This makes the sentinel pattern explicit and type-checkable.

findings.summary: ensure_state_defaults annotated to return None implicitly but has explicit `return None` statement
evidence: Line 219: `def ensure_state_defaults(state: StateModel | dict) -> None:` mutates state in-place. Line 278: `return None` — an explicit return None at the end of a function typed -> None. While not a bug, the explicit return misleadingly suggests there is a code path that returns something else. The function name 'ensure' implies a side-effect-only operation, which is correct.
findings.suggestion: Remove the explicit `return None` on line 278 since the function is -> None and all paths naturally fall through.

findings.summary: Several command entry points use generic parameter name `args` with `getattr` instead of structured access
evidence: scan_workflow.py line 406: `prepare_scan_runtime(args)` uses `getattr(args, 'reset_subjective', False)`, `getattr(args, 'skip_slow', False)`, `getattr(args, 'profile', None)`. preflight.py line 197: `review_rerun_preflight(state, args)` uses `getattr(args, 'force_review_rerun', False)`. scan_preflight.py line 16: `scan_queue_preflight(args)` uses `getattr(args, 'profile', None)`, `getattr(args, 'force_rescan', False)`. These getattr calls with defaults effectively defeat type checking and make the required argument shape invisible.
findings.suggestion: Define typed dataclass or TypedDict for each command's resolved options (e.g. ScanArgs, ReviewArgs) and convert args at the command boundary. This makes the required fields explicit and removes the need for defensive getattr.

findings.summary: Functions with underscore-prefixed names exported in __all__ of show/scope.py
evidence: Lines 232-240: __all__ includes '_detector_names_hint' and '_lookup_dimension_score'. By convention, underscore-prefixed names signal private/internal API, but exporting them in __all__ declares them as public. Callers see contradictory signals about whether these functions are part of the stable API.
findings.suggestion: Either remove the underscore prefix (making them `detector_names_hint` and `lookup_dimension_score`) or remove them from __all__ if they are truly internal.

findings.summary: load_plan returns dict validated to PlanModel shape but requires type: ignore annotation
evidence: Line 75: `return data  # type: ignore[return-value]` — load_plan declares return type PlanModel (TypedDict) but actually returns a plain dict that has been validated by ensure_plan_defaults + validate_plan. The type system cannot verify the dict matches PlanModel's shape, requiring the suppression comment. This pattern means callers get PlanModel type checking but the contract is not statically enforced.
findings.suggestion: Consider having ensure_plan_defaults return a PlanModel via TypedDict constructor, or use cast(PlanModel, data) with a comment explaining the runtime validation. This documents the contract gap more explicitly than a bare type: ignore.

findings.summary: Review command flows mix sys.exit, raise SystemExit, and return-based error handling
evidence: external.py uses sys.exit(1) for session errors (lines 95-99, 104-117, 395-397), sys.exit(2) for argument errors (line 94), raise SystemExit for scan failures (line 551). prepare.py uses sys.exit(1) for zero files (line 101). scan_preflight.py uses sys.exit(1) for queue gate (line 109). Meanwhile, batch_core.py raises ValueError for validation errors. The inconsistency means callers cannot predict whether an error will terminate the process or raise.
findings.suggestion: Establish a clear convention: command entrypoints use sys.exit for user-facing errors; library/intelligence code raises exceptions that command layers catch. Currently the boundary is blurred with sys.exit calls deep in helper functions.

findings.summary: prepare_holistic_review catches 4 exception types for concern generation best-effort
evidence: Lines 413-414: `except (ImportError, AttributeError, TypeError, ValueError) as exc: logger.debug(...)` — this catches nearly every common exception for the concern generation block. A real bug (e.g., TypeError from passing wrong argument types) would be silently downgraded to a debug log, making production issues invisible.
findings.suggestion: Narrow to the specific exceptions that concern generation can legitimately raise (likely ImportError + ValueError). Let TypeError and AttributeError propagate as they typically indicate programming errors.

findings.summary: review and subjective_review detectors mapped to 'Test health' dimension instead of a review-related dimension
evidence: Lines 307-319: DetectorMeta for 'review' has dimension='Test health' and DetectorMeta for 'subjective_review' also has dimension='Test health'. These detectors produce design quality findings from AI code review, not test-related findings. The dimension assignment determines scoring categorization, so review findings incorrectly contribute to the 'Test health' score.
findings.suggestion: Map 'review' and 'subjective_review' to a 'Design coherence' or 'Code quality' dimension that better reflects what these detectors actually measure. The 'concerns' detector already uses 'Design coherence'.

findings.summary: Multi-hop facade chain: output_api re-exports output, discovery_api re-exports source_discovery+file_paths, utils re-exports all of them
evidence: output_api.py is a pure re-export of output.py with identical __all__ (24 lines, zero logic)
discovery_api.py re-exports from source_discovery.py and file_paths.py with identical __all__ (47 lines, zero logic)
utils.py re-exports everything from both facades plus paths_api, tooling, skill_docs (143 lines, two trivial wrappers)
Some callsites import from utils, others from core.output_api, others from core.output directly, creating three import paths for the same symbols
findings.suggestion: Collapse the two intermediate facades (output_api, discovery_api) into their source modules. Make callers import from the actual module. Keep utils.py as a thin deprecation shim only.

findings.summary: do_run_batches accepts 17 callback/function parameters creating an implicit dependency bag instead of a protocol
evidence: do_run_batches signature has 17 named parameters ending in _fn: run_stamp_fn, load_or_prepare_packet_fn, selected_batch_indexes_fn, prepare_run_artifacts_fn, run_codex_batch_fn, execute_batches_fn, collect_batch_results_fn, print_failures_fn, print_failures_and_exit_fn, merge_batch_results_fn, build_import_provenance_fn, do_import_fn, run_followup_scan_fn, safe_write_text_fn, colorize_fn, plus project_root and subagent_runs_dir
_do_run_batches in batch.py constructs these callbacks by wrapping the actual functions with lambdas, adding a second layer of indirection
findings.suggestion: Define a Protocol or dataclass (e.g. BatchRunnerDeps) that groups these callbacks into a single typed dependency object, reducing the function signature and making the contract explicit.

findings.summary: LangRun uses 12 explicit property pairs (getter+setter) plus __getattr__ forwarding for 30+ LangConfig attrs
evidence: Lines 166-252: twelve property pairs each containing only self.state.X getter/setter with no transformation
Lines 254-260: __getattr__ delegates to _FORWARDED_CONFIG_ATTRS (30 entries) on the underlying config
LangRunOverrides dataclass with sentinel _UNSET values on all 11 fields mirrors LangRuntimeState exactly
The override application loop (_apply_lang_overrides) re-dispatches to a coercion function that switches on field name
findings.suggestion: Have LangRun expose state as a public attribute and let callers access runtime fields directly (run.state.zone_map). The property layer adds no validation or transformation.

findings.summary: _is_numeric in types.py duplicates is_numeric in text_utils.py with identical logic
evidence: types.py line 25: def _is_numeric(value): return isinstance(value, int | float) and not isinstance(value, bool)
text_utils.py line 124: def is_numeric(value): return isinstance(value, int | float) and not isinstance(value, bool)
Both perform the exact same bool-excluding numeric check
findings.suggestion: Delete _is_numeric from types.py and import is_numeric from text_utils.py.

findings.summary: PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS catch-all tuple is used as a broad safety net in 11 files, masking distinct failure modes
evidence: PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS = (ImportError, AttributeError, OSError, ValueError, TypeError, KeyError) covers 6 exception types
Used in 27 except clauses across 11 files, often silently swallowing errors with logging.debug or returning None/0
scan_workflow.py uses it to swallow errors during plan reconciliation; queue_progress.py uses it in 4 separate places
A ValueError from actual bad data is indistinguishable from a ValueError from a programming bug
findings.suggestion: Define a specific PlanLoadError exception that plan loading raises. Narrow PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS to (PlanLoadError, OSError) and let programming errors (TypeError, AttributeError, KeyError) propagate.

findings.summary: batch.py's _do_run_batches wraps each runner_helpers function in a lambda to inject dependencies, creating a glue-code layer
evidence: Lines 198-283: _do_run_batches has 85 lines of lambda wrappers that partially apply runner_helpers functions
Each lambda captures module-level constants (ABSTRACTION_SUB_AXES, FOLLOWUP_SCAN_TIMEOUT_SECONDS) and re-passes them
The actual orchestration logic lives in batches.py do_run_batches; batch.py exists only to construct the callback bag
Example: run_codex_batch_fn is a lambda that constructs CodexBatchRunnerDeps from 6 config values and passes through
findings.suggestion: Move the dependency construction into a dataclass or builder that batches.py consumes directly, eliminating the lambda wiring layer in batch.py.

findings.summary: scan_workflow.py _reconcile_plan_post_scan chains 7 boolean-returning helpers with a shared dirty flag pattern
evidence: Lines 185-221: _reconcile_plan_post_scan calls 7 functions sequentially, each returning bool, ORed into 'dirty'
Each helper function (e.g. _apply_plan_reconciliation, _sync_unscored_dimensions) follows identical pattern: call function, check result, print if non-empty, return bool(changes)
The functions _sync_unscored_dimensions and _sync_stale_dimensions have nearly identical structure (lines 110-140)
findings.suggestion: Extract a small reconciliation step protocol that each step implements, then iterate over steps in a loop, accumulating dirty status.

findings.summary: detect_flat_dirs has 13 parameters including 8 numeric thresholds with complex naming
evidence: Lines 70-100: detect_flat_dirs signature has threshold, child_dir_threshold, child_dir_weight, combined_threshold, sparse_parent_child_threshold, sparse_child_file_threshold, sparse_child_count_threshold, sparse_child_ratio_threshold, thin_wrapper_parent_sibling_threshold, thin_wrapper_max_file_count, thin_wrapper_max_child_dir_count, thin_wrapper_names
The thin_wrapper_names parameter duplicates the module-level THIN_WRAPPER_NAMES frozenset
findings.suggestion: Group thresholds into a FlatDirConfig dataclass with sensible defaults, passing a single config object instead of 13 parameters.

findings.summary: Four coercion functions in coercions.py have identical control flow structure differing only in type and comparison
evidence: coerce_positive_int, coerce_positive_float, coerce_non_negative_float, coerce_non_negative_int all follow: check None, check isinstance, try parse, compare to minimum
Lines 6-16 vs 19-29 vs 32-42 vs 45-55: four copies of the same pattern with minor type/comparison changes
findings.suggestion: Extract a generic _coerce_numeric helper parameterized by target type (int/float) and minimum value, then define the four public functions as thin wrappers.

findings.summary: coerce_target_score has defensive None/type checks on its own fallback parameter which is already typed as float
evidence: Lines 10-13: checks is_numeric(fallback) despite the parameter being declared as float with default 95.0
If is_numeric(fallback) is False, falls back to hardcoded 95.0 — this branch is unreachable from typed callers
findings.suggestion: Remove the defensive is_numeric check on the fallback parameter; trust the type annotation.

findings.summary: __all__ in score_update.py exports _print_strict_target_nudge with leading underscore, violating Python convention
evidence: Line 78: __all__ = ['print_score_update', '_print_strict_target_nudge'] exports a private-by-convention function
Similarly, status_cmd.py __all__ lists show_dimension_table, show_focus_suggestion etc. which are imported from status_parts.render, not defined in status_cmd.py
findings.suggestion: Remove leading underscore from the function name if it is intended to be public, or remove it from __all__ if it is private.

findings.summary: state_path is sometimes Path, sometimes str, sometimes None across command modules without consistent coercion
evidence: CommandRuntime.state_path is typed Path | None
command_runtime() coerces str to Path at line 32
But do_import_run and _do_run_batches pass state_file around as untyped positional arg
apply_flow.py line 78 calls state_path(args) which may return str, then passes to load_state and save_state
Inconsistent: some paths use Path objects, others use str, and the conversion happens at different points
findings.suggestion: Standardize on Path for state_path at the CommandRuntime boundary and flow it as Path through all downstream call chains.

findings.summary: Review tests split into batch/, context/, import_scoring/, policy/ subdirectories while deleted files still appear in git status
evidence: Git status shows 17 files deleted from desloppify/tests/review/ and corresponding files added in subdirectories
The restructure is in-flight (files are staged but not committed), creating temporary import confusion
New subdirectories each have an __init__.py, following a different pattern than sibling test dirs (e.g. tests/commands/ has no subdirs)
findings.suggestion: Complete the test reorganization in one commit. Consider whether sibling test directories (tests/commands/, tests/core/, tests/plan/) should follow the same subdirectory pattern for consistency.

findings.summary: 17 instances of '_ = exc' pattern silently swallow caught exceptions without logging or re-raising
evidence: runner_helpers.py has 7 instances of '_ = exc' including lines 250, 276, 883, 997, 1114, 1190, 1291
scan_workflow.py line 81: except OSError as exc: _ = exc; config['needs_rescan'] = True — no logging
entrypoint.py line 30: except (OSError, ValueError, TypeError) as exc: _ = exc — stream reconfiguration failure silenced
The '_ = exc' idiom is an LLM hallmark: it catches exceptions into a variable that is immediately discarded
findings.suggestion: Replace '_ = exc' with either no binding (bare except clause body) when truly ignorable, or logging.debug for operational silence. For non-trivial cases, add a comment explaining why the error is safe to ignore.

findings.summary: batches.py do_run_batches is a single 550-line function with 8 nested closures and inline progress reporting
evidence: do_run_batches spans lines 263-812 (550 lines) — the largest single function in the codebase
Contains 8 nested function definitions (_append_run_log, _run_batch, _make_task, _report_progress, _record_execution_issue, _write_run_summary)
_report_progress alone is 60+ lines handling heartbeat, queued, start, done events with inline print formatting
LLM-generated orchestration pattern: a monolithic function that accumulates all concerns (logging, progress, error handling, result collection, summary writing) rather than composing smaller units
findings.suggestion: Extract the nested closures into methods on a BatchRunOrchestrator class that holds the shared state (batch_status, stall_warned_batches, run_log_path, etc.).

findings.summary: mechanical.py has 13 _build_* functions with identical defensive dict/list guard patterns
evidence: Each builder follows: iterate by_detector.get(name, []), guard isinstance(detail, dict), extract fields with fallback, append to results, sort, slice[:20]
_build_boundary_violations, _build_dead_code, _build_private_crossings, _build_naming_drift all repeat: detail = finding.get('detail', {}); if not isinstance(detail, dict): detail = {}
The pattern is consistent across 13 functions spanning 400 lines, suggesting formulaic generation rather than domain-driven design
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared _extract_findings_by_detector helper that handles the common iteration, detail normalization, and truncation pattern, letting each builder focus on its unique aggregation logic.

findings.summary: Core scan workflow orchestration functions (prepare_scan_runtime, run_scan_generation, merge_scan_results) are only tested via monkeypatched stubs
evidence: prepare_scan_runtime and run_scan_generation are monkeypatched out in every test_cmd_scan.py test, never tested directly
merge_scan_results (lines 528-588) has no direct test; only tested transitively through fully-stubbed cmd_scan tests
_reconcile_plan_post_scan (lines 185-221) has zero test references
findings.suggestion: Add integration-level tests for prepare_scan_runtime and merge_scan_results with real state objects and temporary paths, verifying state mutation, plan reconciliation, and score persistence

findings.summary: Review command tests use module-level mutable globals (PROJECT_ROOT, REVIEW_PACKET_DIR) as test hooks, coupling tests to production module internals
evidence: batch.py lines 31-33 expose PROJECT_ROOT, REVIEW_PACKET_DIR, SUBAGENT_RUNS_DIR as module-level mutable globals explicitly for tests
external.py lines 29-31 duplicate the same pattern with PROJECT_ROOT, REVIEW_PACKET_DIR, EXTERNAL_SESSION_ROOT
These 'backward-compatible override hooks for tests' force tests to patch production module state, creating fragile coupling
findings.suggestion: Replace module-level globals with dependency injection via function parameters or a configuration object. The _runtime_project_root() indirection already exists - make it accept an optional override parameter instead of reading a module global

findings.summary: runner_helpers.py (1494 lines) has limited test coverage for its threading/subprocess orchestration paths
evidence: runner_helpers.py contains complex concurrency: _execute_parallel with ThreadPoolExecutor, _drain_stream, _write_live_snapshot, heartbeat monitoring
_execute_parallel (lines 945-1074) has no direct test - only test_execute_batches_* tests exist which are at a higher level
collect_batch_results (lines 1140-1192) and _extract_payload_from_log (lines 1098-1137) handle I/O and error recovery but have minimal direct tests
findings.suggestion: Add direct unit tests for _execute_parallel with mock tasks that exercise timeout, stall recovery, and heartbeat callback paths. Test collect_batch_results with various failure/recovery scenarios using temp files

findings.summary: PROJECT_ROOT resolution pattern is duplicated across 4+ review modules with identical _runtime_project_root(), _review_packet_dir(), _blind_packet_path() functions
evidence: batch.py lines 53-72: _runtime_project_root(), _review_packet_dir(), _subagent_runs_dir(), _blind_packet_path()
external.py lines 38-57: _runtime_project_root(), _review_packet_dir(), _external_session_root(), _blind_packet_path()
import_helpers.py line 129: _default_blind_packet_path() - yet another variant
cli.py line 26: PROJECT_ROOT = get_project_root() with _PROJECT_ROOT_SENTINEL for test patching
findings.suggestion: Consolidate path resolution into a single ReviewPathResolver class or module that all review subcommands import. This eliminates 4 copies of _runtime_project_root and 3 copies of _blind_packet_path

findings.summary: do_external_submit has 10 parameters including 4 boolean flags, indicating the function interface should be decomposed
evidence: do_external_submit (line 471) signature: import_file, session_id, state, lang, state_file, config, allow_partial, scan_after_import, scan_path, dry_run
do_import_run (batch.py line 327) has 8 parameters with the same pattern: state, lang, state_file, config, allow_partial, scan_after_import, scan_path
_run_review_mode (entrypoint.py line 102) passes 6 params to call these functions, each with different boolean combinations
findings.suggestion: Introduce a ReviewImportOptions dataclass bundling allow_partial, scan_after_import, scan_path, and dry_run. Pass this object instead of individual boolean flags to all review import/submit functions

findings.summary: Review entrypoint uses getattr for 7+ boolean flags with inconsistent bool() wrapping, creating fragile dispatch logic
evidence: entrypoint.py _mode_flags (lines 43-61): 7 separate getattr() calls to extract mode booleans, some wrapped in bool(), some not
_run_review_mode (lines 102-186): 10 getattr() calls with bool() casts, string conversions, and None defaults spread across the dispatch chain
Each mode branch passes a different subset of args extracted via getattr, making it hard to verify completeness
findings.suggestion: Define a ReviewModeArgs dataclass parsed once from argparse.Namespace at the top of cmd_review, then pass the typed object to _run_review_mode. This centralizes validation and eliminates scattered getattr calls

findings.summary: desloppify/utils.py is a deprecated facade (planned removal 2026-09-30) but is still imported by 8+ production-adjacent modules
evidence: utils.py line 1: 'Deprecated compatibility facade for legacy imports'
utils.py line 11: 'Planned removal: 2026-09-30'
8+ test files still import desloppify.utils: test_naming.py, test_ts_deprecated.py, test_ts_deps.py, test_utils.py, test_holistic_review.py, test_csharp_extractors.py, test_csharp_init.py
test_transitive_modules.py imports SKILL_BEGIN, SKILL_END, SkillInstall from desloppify.utils (lines 712-782) rather than from desloppify.core.skill_docs
findings.suggestion: Migrate remaining test imports to use the focused public APIs (desloppify.core.paths_api, discovery_api, output_api, skill_docs). Track migration progress in a test to prevent regression

findings.summary: LEGACY_DIMENSION_NOTE_ISSUES_KEY and LEGACY_REVIEW_QUALITY_HIGH_SCORE_MISSING_ISSUES_KEY are actively used by production batch_core.py, not just retained for compatibility
evidence: feedback_contract.py lines 9,11: defines LEGACY_DIMENSION_NOTE_ISSUES_KEY='unreported_risk' and LEGACY_REVIEW_QUALITY_HIGH_SCORE_MISSING_ISSUES_KEY='high_score_without_risk'
batch_core.py line 154: falls back to LEGACY_DIMENSION_NOTE_ISSUES_KEY when the canonical key is missing
batches.py line 75: actively uses LEGACY_REVIEW_QUALITY_HIGH_SCORE_MISSING_ISSUES_KEY in quality scoring logic
HIGH_SCORE_UNREPORTED_RISK_THRESHOLD (line 16) is a backward-compat alias exported in __all__ but has zero non-test callers
findings.suggestion: Either complete the migration by removing old key fallbacks and requiring the canonical keys, or document these as permanent dual-key support rather than labeling them LEGACY. Remove HIGH_SCORE_UNREPORTED_RISK_THRESHOLD since it has no callers

findings.summary: Backward-compatible aliases in python/phases.py and tool_factories.py have no remaining callers
evidence: python/phases.py lines 369-378: 9 aliases (_phase_unused = phase_unused, etc.) annotated 'Backward-compatible aliases for internal callers/tests'
grep for 'from desloppify.languages.python.phases import _phase_' returns zero results - no remaining callers
tool_factories.py lines 162-163: make_detect_fn_with_runner = make_detect_fn, make_generic_fixer_with_runner = make_generic_fixer - no callers besides the definition
These aliases add dead symbols to the module's public surface
findings.suggestion: Remove all 11 unused backward-compatible aliases from python/phases.py and tool_factories.py since no caller imports them

findings.summary: dimensions/metadata.py maintains 4 parallel hardcoded LEGACY data structures alongside the JSON-driven dimension system
evidence: metadata.py lines 19-57: _LEGACY_DISPLAY_NAMES, _LEGACY_SUBJECTIVE_WEIGHTS_BY_DISPLAY, _LEGACY_RESET_ON_SCAN_DIMENSIONS, _LEGACY_WEIGHT_BY_DIMENSION are all hardcoded dicts/frozensets
Lines 191-197: 'Preserve legacy dimensions even if a payload temporarily drops one' - the legacy data is merged back as a safety net after all JSON processing
Lines 177-188: every dimension falls back to _LEGACY_DISPLAY_NAMES and _LEGACY_WEIGHT_BY_DIMENSION when JSON data is incomplete
The JSON-driven system (load_dimensions, load_dimensions_for_lang) should be the single source of truth but the legacy constants override it
findings.suggestion: Promote the JSON dimension files to be the canonical source and remove the hardcoded _LEGACY_* constants. If backward compatibility is needed, validate that JSON files contain all required dimensions at build/test time rather than maintaining parallel data

findings.summary: External review session tokens stored as plain text in JSON files on disk
evidence: Session tokens (e.g. session.token in review_result.template.json) are stored unencrypted in .desloppify/ directory. While this is a CLI tool with no network auth surface, the tokens are used for session integrity verification.
findings.suggestion: Consider documenting that session tokens are local-only integrity checks, not security credentials, to avoid confusion.

findings.summary: Optional dependency extras (tree-sitter, etc.) imported without clear availability checks in some paths
evidence: The project has zero runtime dependencies (excellent), but some optional extras like tree-sitter are imported with try/except blocks scattered across files rather than a centralized availability check.
findings.suggestion: Centralize optional dependency availability into a single module (e.g. core/optional_deps.py) that each consumer checks.
